questions.answer: Organisational structure refers to the framework or pattern of relationships among various components of an organisation.
questions.topic: Need and Importance of Organisational Structure

questions.answer: Bureaucracy refers to a system of management where every decision is made at a higher level and is impersonal.

questions.q: Mention any two advantages of functional structure.
questions.answer: Separation of specialised functions, Easy coordination and control.
Matrix structure refers to a structure where an employee reports to more than one superior for different tasks.
key_points: Reports to more than one superior

questions.q: What is the main advantage of divisional structure?
questions.answer: It enables effective control and coordination of a large organisation.

questions.q: Compare and contrast functional and divisional structures.
questions.answer: Functional and divisional structures differ in their approach to organisation. Functional structure is based on specialisation, whereas divisional structure is based on market or product.
key_points: Divisional structure based on market or product

questions.q: Explain the need for an organisational structure.
questions.answer: Organisational structure is essential for achieving organisational goals and objectives. It helps in defining roles and responsibilities, and facilitates coordination and control.
questions.topic: Need and Importance of Organisational Structure

questions.q: Describe the characteristics of a bureaucratic structure.
questions.answer: A bureaucratic structure is characterised by a hierarchical structure, division of work, rules and procedures, impersonal relationships, and promotion based on seniority.

questions.q: What are the advantages and disadvantages of a functional structure?
questions.answer: Advantages: Separation of specialised functions, Easy coordination and control. Disadvantages: Lack of flexibility, Inefficient communication.

questions.q: Mention the features of a matrix structure.
questions.answer: Features of a matrix structure include multiple reporting relationships, project-based teams, and flexible job assignments.

questions.q: Compare the organisational structures of a small and large organisation.
questions.answer: Small organisations have a flat structure with minimal layers, whereas large organisations have a hierarchical structure with multiple layers. Small organisations have a simple structure, while large organisations have a complex structure.
questions.topic: Organisational Structure of Small and Large Organisations

questions.q: Explain the role of the line and staff in an organisational structure.
questions.answer: The line is responsible for the main activities of the organisation, while the staff provides support and advice to the line. The line is responsible for decision-making, while the staff is responsible for providing expertise and guidance.
questions.topic: Line and Staff in Organisational Structure

questions.q: Describe the different types of organisational structures.
questions.answer: The different types of organisational structures include functional, divisional, matrix, and bureaucratic structures. Each structure has its own strengths and weaknesses, and is suited to different types of organisations and environments.

questions.q: What are the benefits and drawbacks of a divisional structure?
questions.answer: Benefits: Enables effective control and coordination of a large organisation, Facilitates communication and decision-making. Drawbacks: Can be complex and difficult to manage, May lead to duplication of efforts.

questions.q: Compare and contrast the advantages and disadvantages of a functional and divisional structure.
questions.answer: Functional structure: Advantages - Separation of specialised functions, Easy coordination and control. Disadvantages - Lack of flexibility, Inefficient communication. Divisional structure: Advantages - Enables effective control and coordination of a large organisation, Facilitates communication and decision-making. Disadvantages - Can be complex and difficult to manage, May lead to duplication of efforts.

questions.q: Explain the importance of flexibility in an organisational structure.
questions.answer: Flexibility is essential in an organisational structure as it enables the organisation to adapt to changing circumstances and environments. It allows for faster decision-making and response to changes, and enables the organisation to stay competitive.
questions.topic: Importance of Flexibility in Organisational Structure
